      Fund.com
   =20
 =20
Fund.com was, for a while, apparently a scam. It was founded in 2004 as Eas=
tern Services Holdings Inc., =E2=80=9Cone of many entities that Imran Husai=
n formed and took public to sell to market manipulators.=E2=80=9D
  [1]
 In 2007, it was sold to Jason Galanis, a market manipulator who had recent=
ly been banned by the US Securities and Exchange Commission from serving as=
 a public-company officer or director. (Galanis is currently in prison for =
an assortment of=C2=A0other=C2=A0frauds, and is somehow involved in a Hunte=
r Biden situation.) Under Galanis, the company acquired the web address=C2=
=A0=E2=80=9Cwww.fund.com,=E2=80=9D changed its name to Fund.com Inc., and t=
old people the address was worth $10 million. =E2=80=9CThe Website Address =
certainly had some value, but Galanis appears to have manufactured the $10 =
million figure through fraudulent transactions.=E2=80=9DAnd then Fund.com w=
ent around doing fraud to sell stock to retail investors =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=
=80=9CGalanis paid kickbacks to financial advisors to get them to invest th=
eir client portfolios in the Company,=E2=80=9D etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0and rais=
ed money. With some of that money, Fund.com struck a deal to pay $4 million=
 for a 60% stake in AdvisorShares Investments LLC, a perfectly legitimate a=
ctive exchange-traded fund manager. I am not sure how that happened, but by=
 2010 AdvisorShares regretted it: =E2=80=9CAfter the Company=E2=80=99s prob=
lems, the ETF Provider had no interest in the Company remaining an investor=
 and sought to cancel the Company=E2=80=99s equity stake.=E2=80=9D Meanwhil=
e Fund.com=E2=80=99s stock fell from a high of $570 per share to a low of s=
ix cents,
  [2]
=C2=A0and it stopped filing its securities reports. And Galanis =E2=80=9Ccr=
eated a new entity named Fund Alliance Corporation that purported to buy th=
e Website Address for $1.5 million,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Cfor use in a crowd-fu=
nding scheme.=E2=80=9DAt this point, the company had an equity market capit=
alization of something like $100,000, and an investor named Thomas Braziel=
=C2=A0saw an opportunity. The opportunity was that this company=C2=A0looked=
=C2=A0like the spent husk of a series of stock scams, but it=C2=A0actually =
had assets. Maybe. The assets were (1) the web address=C2=A0(if it could ge=
t it back from Galanis) and (2) the 60% stake in AdvisorShares (if it could=
 prevent AdvisorShares from canceling that). So Braziel bought about 20% of=
 the company for about $20,000, and then tried to crack it open to take ove=
r the assets.Classically the ways to do that include doing a tender offer f=
or all of the shares, running a proxy fight to take over the board, stuff l=
ike that. But given what a spent husk Fund.com was, Braziel did something e=
lse: He petitioned a Delaware court to appoint him as receiver of the compa=
ny. Basically, he went to court and said =E2=80=9CI am a shareholder of thi=
s company, this company is defunct, you should put me in charge so that I c=
an get some value out of it for myself and the other shareholders.=E2=80=9D=
 This worked, in part because the company really was so defunct that it nev=
er responded to his petition:The petition sought to have Braziel appointed =
as a liquidating receiver for the Company under Section 226(a)(3) of the DG=
CL. That statute authorizes a court to appoint a receiver when =E2=80=9Cthe=
 corporation has abandoned its business and has failed within a reasonable =
time to take steps to dissolve, liquidate or distribute its assets.=E2=80=
=9D 8 Del. C. =C2=A7 226(a)(3).When the Company did not respond to the peti=
tion, the Investment Fund moved for a default judgment. The motion emphasiz=
ed that Braziel was a qualified person who had studied the rules governing =
receivers of Delaware corporations.By order dated November 29, 2016, the co=
urt entered a default judgment and appointed Braziel as a receiver. The ord=
er charged Braziel with liquidating the Company and distributing its net as=
sets to its investors. The order did not authorize Braziel to conduct busin=
ess through the Company.Having cracked open the company, Braziel succeeded =
in getting money out of it. He sued Fund Alliance and got the web address b=
ack for $750,000. He renegotiated a settlement with AdvisorShares in which =
Fund.com got a couple of million dollars in exchange for canceling its Advi=
sorShares stake. Meanwhile, =E2=80=9Con=C2=A0paper, the Company owed over $=
8 million to Galanis=E2=80=99s affiliates,=E2=80=9D and Braziel succeeded i=
n getting that debt canceled because Galanis forgot about it:On December 22=
, [Braziel] moved for an order requiring that the Company=E2=80=99s credito=
rs make any claims against the receivership estate by April 14, 2017. The c=
ourt approved the order and set a bar date for asserting claims. Galanis an=
d his associates failed to assert timely claims, which prevented them asser=
ting any claims based on their purported $8 million in debt. Braziel disall=
owed claims for two other potential creditors for another $1.5 million.All =
very nice work! Braziel now controlled a company that had little debt and s=
ome real money. What should he do with it? The good answer is =E2=80=9Cretu=
rn the money to the shareholders=C2=A0pro rata, getting 20% himself.=E2=80=
=9D The actual answer is some combination of =E2=80=9Ctake the money himsel=
f=E2=80=9D:Over the next eight months, Braziel caused the Company to wire a=
 total of $121,000 to his personal checking account in eighteen installment=
s. Braziel never disclosed the transfers to the court or his attorneys.And =
=E2=80=9CYOLO some call options=E2=80=9D:Braziel next decided to use the Co=
mpany=E2=80=99s money to fund high-risk, high-reward investments. Using a c=
ombination of money he extracted from the Company, plus some funds received=
 from family members, Braziel invested in common stock and call options on =
the stock of Belmond, Ltd., a Bermudian hotelier. Between July 12 and 18, 2=
018, the Company wired $315,000 to Braziel=E2=80=99s checking account. On J=
uly 17, Braziel opened a brokerage account with Interactive Brokers, LLC (t=
he =E2=80=9CFirst=C2=A0Brokerage Account=E2=80=9D). On July 18 and 19, Braz=
iel moved $270,000 from his checking account to the First Brokerage Account=
.Starting on July 18, 2018, Braziel used the funds in the First Brokerage A=
ccount to buy and sell common stock and call options for Belmond=E2=80=99s =
stock.This all went well enough that, =E2=80=9Chaving seen that he could us=
e the Company=E2=80=99s assets to make investments, Braziel filed a motion =
on October 16, 2018, that asked the court to discontinue the liquidation an=
d approve the Company=E2=80=99s re-emergence as a publicly traded investmen=
t vehicle.=E2=80=9D The company is back in business. Also all of his trades=
 were good? The call options turned out to be on a merger target,=C2=A0and =
he sold the web address for more than he paid for it:Two months after the e=
ntry of the Discontinuation Order, Braziel=E2=80=99s investment in Belmond =
paid off. The bulk of the position consisted of out-of-the-money call optio=
ns expiring on December 21. Just in time, on December 14, LVMH Mo=C3=ABt He=
nnessy=E2=80=94Louis Vuitton SE announced an acquisition of Belmond=E2=80=
=99s public equity at $25 per share. Braziel sold the call options for $936=
,344.77, generating a gain of $832,464.61. In total, the value of the Belmo=
nd Investment amounted to approximately $1,494,337.06. Braziel also sold th=
e Website Address. In December 2018, a third party agreed to buy the Websit=
e Address for $1.5 million. After a 15% sales commission, the Company nette=
d $1.275 million, resulting in a gain of $525,000 over the $750,000 the Com=
pany paid to re-acquire the Website Address in 2017.Good! But also bad:Braz=
iel helped himself to the proceeds. Between December 20, 2018, and February=
 13, 2019, the Company wired $1.65 million to Braziel=E2=80=99s checking ac=
count. Braziel spent $347,786.43 on a sapphire ring and a pair of emerald-a=
nd-diamond earrings from Lorraine Schwartz, $61,355.31 on a German watch, a=
nd over $400,000 on luxury hotel stays, apparel, art, and other fineries. B=
raziel invested the rest of his takings in bankruptcy claims, cryptocurrenc=
y, leveraged loans, and high-risk equities. Many of these investments produ=
ced outside gains. Despite having obtained the gains using Company funds, B=
raziel did not share the gains with the Company.=C2=A0Eventually =E2=80=9Ca=
 longtime stockholder of the Company who spent part of his career as a fede=
ral prosecutor =E2=80=A6=C2=A0began to wonder what Braziel was doing.=E2=80=
=9D So he asked, did not get a satisfactory answer, and sent a letter to th=
e Delaware court that had supervised the liquidation, accusing Braziel of =
=E2=80=9Cembezzl[ing] three million dollars from the shareholders.=E2=80=9D=
 The court appointed a special magistrate to investigate, and the magistrat=
e concluded that Braziel had in fact taken the money and should pay back ab=
out $2 million. Braziel was apparently like =E2=80=9Cfair play, you got me=
=E2=80=9D:To the receiver=E2=80=99s [Braziel=E2=80=99s] credit, he largely =
acknowledged the accuracy of the special magistrate=E2=80=99s recommended f=
actual findings. He also accepted aspects of the special magistrate=E2=80=

osts of the special magistrate=E2=80=99s investigation.He raised some techn=
ical objections, though, and last week Delaware Vice Chancellor Travis Last=
er decided he should actually pay back about $1.95 million. The quotes abov=
e are from Vice Chancellor Laster=E2=80=99s opinion.I suppose the moral of =
the story is something like: If you go around hunting for defunct scams tha=

e money for yourself. =E2=80=9CAhh, this thing was a scam anyway=E2=80=9D y=
ou think; =E2=80=9Cany value that I can salvage is just found money and I s=
hould keep it.=E2=80=9D But you are not actually allowed to do that! Even w=

 buying claims in high-profile cryptocurrency bankruptcy cases from custome=

 Fund.com does seem like good practice for trading FTX claims.

   =20
      M&A fees
   =20
 =20
Investment bankers mostly work for free, and are sometimes lavishly overpai=
d. If you are the chief executive officer of a big company, and you want ad=
vice about potential acquisition targets, or modeling for potential financi=
ng transactions, or a recommendation for a new chief financial officer, you=
 can call up your friendly investment banker and say =E2=80=9Chey no rush o=
r anything but I was wondering about ...=E2=80=9D and she will send you a b=
eautifully formatted 40-page analysis by the next morning. You would never =
think of paying for this, and she would never ask. But then if you ever=C2=
=A0do=C2=A0acquire a company or raise financing, you will feel some obligat=
ion to call her, and she will charge you millions of dollars for that.=C2=
=A0This works fine, but it has to be carefully calibrated. If a bank builds=
 its business on a model like =E2=80=9C10% of our clients will pursue deals=
, and 50% of those deals will sign, and 80% of those deals will close,=E2=
=80=9D then it can figure that it will get paid for 4% of its work and char=
ge 25 times as much as that work costs, to cover all of the unpaid work.
  [3]
 But then if the antitrust regime changes and only 60% of signed deals clos=
e, you are underpaid.At Reuters, Anirban Sen reports:Bankers have been push=
ing to get paid even when a deal is thwarted by regulators, and are chargin=
g more for services paid irrespective of whether a transaction closes, inte=
rviews with more than a dozen dealmakers showed.The banks' tactics include =
taking a larger slice of the breakup fee paid by the acquirer to the target=
 for failing to close a deal, and charging more for "fairness opinions" the=
y provide to companies on whether they should sell themselves. =E2=80=A6U.S=
. antitrust regulators filed 50 enforcement actions against mergers in the =
12 months to the end of September 2022, representing the highest level of e=
nforcement activity in over 20 years, according to the most recent data pub=
lished by the Federal Trade Commission and U.S. Department of Justice. =E2=
=80=A6Top investment banks, including Goldman Sachs (GS.N), opens new tab, =
JPMorgan Chase (JPM.N), opens new tab and Morgan Stanley (MS.N), opens new =
tab, are pushing to be paid as much as 25% of the breakup fee on some trans=
actions, depending on the transaction's size, according to the dealmakers w=
ho were interviewed. That is up from a historic average of receiving about =
15% of the breakup fee, they added. =E2=80=A6Investment banks have also bee=
n making roughly 20-25% of their advisory fees to companies selling themsel=
ves subject to delivering fairness opinions, which are paid even if a deal =
does not close. Referred to in the industry as "announcement" fees, these a=
re up from an average of 5% to 6% of the total advisory fees during the pre=
vious decade, according to several dealmakers and regulatory filings.It wou=
ld be funny if the bankers explicitly said =E2=80=9Cthe only way we can kee=
p our prices low is by also charging for busted deals,=E2=80=9D but they do=
n=E2=80=99t, probably because it=E2=80=99s hard to argue that their prices =
are low.

   =20
      Hedge funds
   =20
 =20
A few months ago, I mentioned =E2=80=9CBill Ackman=E2=80=99s old-school app=
roach to managing a hedge fund =E2=80=94 he=E2=80=99s a famous guy, he make=
s concentrated high-conviction bets on stocks, he doesn=E2=80=99t hedge muc=
h,=E2=80=9D and a reader wrote in to chastise me. The=C2=A0old-school=C2=A0=
approach to managing a hedge fund is Alfred Winslow Jones=E2=80=99s: Jones =
invented the hedge fund, and his early approach was arguably more like that=
 of modern multistrategy funds that hire lots of smart investors to pick st=
ocks and hedge out market exposure. (Thus the name.) Jones also invented th=
e hedge fund compensation scheme, though (particularly the 20% performance =
fee), and=C2=A0that proliferated among funds that didn=E2=80=99t do as much=
 hedging. But a famous guy making big stock bets and charging 20% of return=
s was,=C2=A0in the long history of hedge funds, something of a novelty.Stil=
l, I stand by what I wrote: There was=C2=A0a fairly long vogue for high-pro=
file individual hedge fund managers who made big bets, and that era is pass=
ing, and now hedge funds are big institutions stocked with well-paid but so=
mewhat anonymous portfolio managers grinding out alpha with low volatility.=
 At Business Insider, Linette Lopez meditates on the transition:What is cle=
ar is that hedge funds run by an individual star, by one prodigious mind, a=
re not raising the massive money they used to. Clients who once were proud =
to hand their money to a specific person are pushing back; they want to pay=
 lower fees and see less-volatile returns. The funds that have survived rel=
y on a stable of faceless traders testing out different ideas, brokering tr=
ansactions, and harvesting the returns for the collective. Quant strategies=
 =E2=80=94 which are built on algorithmic trading =E2=80=94 have also becom=
e more popular with the ultrawealthy. More robots, fewer people, lower over=
head. =E2=80=A6In decades past, clients were mostly HNWIs: high-net-worth i=
ndividuals. Nowadays the biggest money runners are institutions, and instit=
utions are more exacting. One legendary but shy fund manager told me that t=
hese institutions are more interested in steady returns, and the wild swing=
s in performance that hedge funds offer simply give them heartburn. "They c=
an sleep better at night," the person said. "The economy can turn bad, the =
stock market can crash, and they're not going to share losses."Sebastian Ma=
llaby, the author of "More Money Than God: Hedge Funds and the Making of th=
e New Elite," told me that institutions are also less willing to be enchant=
ed by the personal magnetism of a founder like Julian Robertson, the founde=
r of the legendary fund Tiger Management."In the old days, Julian Robertson=
 would hold a party for HNWIs and have some kind of wacky show for them at =
the Met, and they'd say 'Julian, what a guy' and leave him alone for a year=
," Mallaby said. "That's gone away because individual investors aren't as i=
mportant as institutional investors, who have to show they've been careful =
where they put the money.=E2=80=9DThe story here is something like =E2=80=
=9Cinstitutions demand that they actually get identifiable alpha and uncorr=
elated, high and stable returns in exchange for all the fees they are payin=
g hedge funds, and the hedge fund industry is responding by building stable=
 professional institutions that actually provide that service.=E2=80=9D Tha=
t=E2=80=99s a good story! But the parties might be less fun.

      Socialist calculation problem
   =20
 =20
Theoretically, there are about two good ways to run an economy:=09Free mark=
ets. People can make stuff and buy whatever they want, and markets and pric=
e signals sort out how much gets made and who gets it. If people want widge=
ts, and I make widgets, I can sell the widgets for money, which will make m=
e want to make widgets. If nobody wants sprockets, and I make sprockets, so=
on I will stop and do something more useful.=C2=A0=09A big computer. You pr=
ogram a big computer to figure out everyone=E2=80=99s preferences and marsh=
al all the available resources, and then you solve a complicated optimizati=
on problem to figure out how best to use the resources to meet the preferen=
ces.=C2=A0The first approach has been pretty widely adopted, in variously m=
odified forms, over many years in many places, and has a decent if not unbl=
emished record of allocating resources and satisfying demand. The second ap=
proach has=C2=A0some=C2=A0track record =E2=80=94=C2=A0I have at least once =
mentioned Francis Spufford=E2=80=99s terrific historical novel,=C2=A0Red Pl=
enty, about Soviet efforts in this vein =E2=80=94=C2=A0but there are not a =
lot of clean examples of it working at economy-wide scale.=C2=A0You would n=
eed a pretty big computer and it seems hard.Still I am not sure that it is=
=C2=A0refuted? It=E2=80=99s more, like, Real Big Computers Have Never Been =
Tried. You could tell a story like:=09Computers are really good now. There =
have been rapid advances in processing power and=C2=A0machine learning and =
artificial intelligence, so you should expect big computers to have a bette=
r chance of running an economy in 2024 =E2=80=94=C2=A0or 2044 =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0than they did in 1964.=09One outcome of free markets seems to be the ris=
e of larger companies, and the larger and more diversified a company is, th=
e less it will be governed by price signals: If I mine my own ore and refin=
e my own steel to make widgets, and then use those widgets in my gizmo fact=
ory, my decisions about how many widgets to make will be driven not by the =
price of steel or widgets, but by how many widgets the VP of Gizmo Factorie=
s wants and how much steel the VP of Steel Milling can provide.=C2=A0=09Ind=
ex funds? When I mentioned=C2=A0Red Plenty, it was in the context of writin=
g about a Sanford C. Bernstein & Co. research note arguing that =E2=80=9CPa=
ssive Investing is Worse Than Marxism=E2=80=9D because passive investors do=
 not try to allocate capital to its best uses, but just buy whatever is in =
the index.=C2=A0If that were true =E2=80=94=C2=A0if financial markets have =
abdicated their responsibility to allocate resources using price signals =
=E2=80=94=C2=A0you=E2=80=99d need some sort of allocation mechanism to repl=
ace it.Anyway this is all pretty idle speculation but here=E2=80=99s a fun =
Wall Street Journal article about Amazon.com=E2=80=99s smart speaker busine=
ss:When Amazon launched the Echo smart home devices with its Alexa voice as=
sistant in 2014, it pulled a page from shaving giant Gillette=E2=80=99s cla=
ssic playbook: sell the razors for a pittance in the hope of making heaps o=
f money on purchases of the refill blades.A decade later, the payoff for Ec=
ho hasn=E2=80=99t arrived. While hundreds of millions of customers have Ale=
xa-enabled devices, the idea that people would spend meaningful amounts of =
money to buy goods on Amazon by talking to the iconic voice assistant on th=
e underpriced speakers didn=E2=80=99t take off.Customers actually used Echo=
 mostly for free apps such as setting alarms and checking the weather. =E2=
=80=9CWe worried we=E2=80=99ve hired 10,000 people and we=E2=80=99ve built =
a smart timer,=E2=80=9D said a former senior employee.=C2=A0=E2=80=A6As [Ch=
ief Executive Officer Andy] Jassy tries to fix it, he is rethinking the obs=
cure Bezos-era metric inside Amazon that helps explain why Echo and other d=
evices could accrue such huge losses for so long with little repercussion. =
Called =E2=80=9Cdownstream impact,=E2=80=9D or DSI, it assigns a financial =
value to a product or a service based on how customers spend within Amazon=
=E2=80=99s ecosystem after they buy it. ...The metric was developed in 2011=
 by a team of economists including an eventual Nobel Prize winner. In some =
instances, the model worked clearly. When customers buy Amazon=E2=80=99s Ki=
ndle e-reader=E2=80=94one of Amazon=E2=80=99s profitable devices=E2=80=94th=
ey are very likely to then buy ebooks to read on that device. Ebooks are pa=
rt of the books business, not the devices business, but Amazon leaders said=
 it made sense for the Kindle team to claim part of revenue when assessing =
their product=E2=80=99s internal value. ...In other cases=E2=80=94especiall=
y Echo devices=E2=80=94the downstream impact idea broke down, said the peop=
le familiar with the devices business.=C2=A0If you were in the business of =
selling smart speakers, you would try to (1) sell them for more than it cos=
t to make them and/or (2) negotiate some sort of arm=E2=80=99s-length partn=
erships with advertisers, business partners, etc., who might pay you for ac=
cess to your smart speakers=E2=80=99 customers. If the amount of money that=
 customers paid, plus the amount of money that advertisers/partners paid, e=
xceeded the cost of making the speaker, you=E2=80=99d do it. If not, you=E2=
=80=99d stop. Capitalism!But Amazon is not in the business of selling smart=
 speakers. It=E2=80=99s in the business of selling=C2=A0everything, which i=
s harder. If you sell everything, your price signals get obscured. If Amazo=
n just gave everyone a phone, for free, with the Amazon app on it, that wou=
ld probably increase Amazon=E2=80=99s sales of books and toilet paper and e=
lectronics and dog food and video subscriptions. But would it be worth it? =
For that you need a model developed by a team of economists. And sometimes =
the model doesn=E2=80=99t work.

<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Fund.com</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Fund.com was, for a while, apparently =
a scam. It was founded in 2004 as Eastern Services Holdings Inc., =E2=80=9C=
one of many entities that Imran Husain formed and took public to sell to ma=
rket manipulators.=E2=80=9D<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1"=
 style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !importa=
nt; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a> In 2007, it was sold to Jason Galanis, a market manipulator who had re=
cently been banned by the US Securities and Exchange Commission from servin=
g as a public-company officer or director. (Galanis is <a href=3D"https://l=
ink.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36159276.193394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuanVzdG=
ljZS5nb3YvdXNhby1zZG55L3ByL2phc29uLWdhbGFuaXMtc2VudGVuY2VkLW1hbmhhdHRhbi1mZ=
WRlcmFsLWNvdXJ0LW11bHRpcGxlLXNlY3VyaXRpZXMtZnJhdWQtc2NoZW1lcw/607f07ceef4b8=
524a319a23cBbc6fd6c3" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;">currently in prison</a> for an assortment of=
=C2=A0<em>other=C2=A0</em>frauds, and is somehow involved in a <a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36159276.193394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3=
cuYXhpb3MuY29tLzIwMjQvMDMvMTgvaG91c2UtcmVwdWJsaWNhbnMtaHVudGVyLWJpZGVuLWhlY=
XJpbmctcHJpc29u/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB802adc82" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" i=
temscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-deco=
ration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #00=
0000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Hunter Biden sit=
uation</a>.) Under Galanis, the company acquired the web address=C2=A0=E2=
=80=9Cwww.fund.com,=E2=80=9D changed its name to Fund.com Inc., and told pe=
ople the address was worth $10 million. =E2=80=9CThe Website Address certai=
nly had some value, but Galanis appears to have manufactured the $10 millio=
n figure through fraudulent transactions.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 1=
6px 0;">And then Fund.com went around doing fraud to sell stock to retail i=
nvestors =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9CGalanis paid kickbacks to financial adviso=
rs to get them to invest their client portfolios in the Company,=E2=80=9D e=
tc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0and raised money. With some of that money, Fund.com <a h=
ref=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36159276.193394/aHR0cH=
M6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZGdhci9kYXRhLzEzMzU3OTUvMDAwMTIxMzkwMDA4M=
DAyMTYxL2Y4azExMDYwOF9mdW5kLmh0bQ/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB41bd0f08" itempr=
op=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: =
#000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border=
-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;=
">struck a deal</a> to pay $4 million for a 60% stake in AdvisorShares Inve=
stments LLC, a perfectly legitimate active exchange-traded fund manager. I =
am not sure how that happened, but by 2010 AdvisorShares regretted it: =E2=
=80=9CAfter the Company=E2=80=99s problems, the ETF Provider had no interes=
t in the Company remaining an investor and sought to cancel the Company=E2=
=80=99s equity stake.=E2=80=9D Meanwhile Fund.com=E2=80=99s stock fell from=
 a high of $570 per share to a low of six cents,<a class=3D"footnote-index"=
 href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display:=
 inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>=C2=A0and it stopped filing its securities reports. And Galanis =E2=80=
=9Ccreated a new entity named Fund Alliance Corporation that purported to b=
uy the Website Address for $1.5 million,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Cfor use in a cro=
wd-funding scheme.=E2=80=9D</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At this point, =
the company had an equity market capitalization of something like $100,000,=
 and an investor named Thomas Braziel=C2=A0saw an opportunity. The opportun=
ity was that this company=C2=A0<em>looked=C2=A0</em>like the spent husk of =
a series of stock scams, but it=C2=A0<em>actually had assets</em>. Maybe. T=
he assets were (1) the web address=C2=A0(if it could get it back from Galan=
is) and (2) the 60% stake in AdvisorShares (if it could prevent AdvisorShar=
es from canceling that). So Braziel <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergb=
usiness.com/click/36159276.193394/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9BcmNoaXZlcy9lZ=
Gdhci9kYXRhLzEzMzU3OTUvMDAwMTA2Mjk5MzE2MDEwNDQwL3NjaGVkMTNkLmh0bQ/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cBec8b5aa7" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" ta=
rget=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; =
border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style=
: solid; background-color: #ccc;">bought about 20%</a> of the company for a=
bout $20,000, and then tried to crack it open to take over the assets.</p><=
p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Classically the ways to do that include doing a=
 tender offer for all of the shares, running a proxy fight to take over the=
 board, stuff like that. But given what a spent husk Fund.com was, Braziel =
did something else: He petitioned a Delaware court to appoint him as receiv=
er of the company. Basically, he went to court and said =E2=80=9CI am a sha=
reholder of this company, this company is defunct, you should put me in cha=
rge so that I can get some value out of it for myself and the other shareho=
lders.=E2=80=9D This worked, in part because the company really was so defu=
nct that it never responded to his petition:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"mar=
gin: 16px 0;">The petition sought to have Braziel appointed as a liquidatin=
g receiver for the Company under Section 226(a)(3) of the DGCL. That statut=
e authorizes a court to appoint a receiver when =E2=80=9Cthe corporation ha=
s abandoned its business and has failed within a reasonable time to take st=
eps to dissolve, liquidate or distribute its assets.=E2=80=9D 8 Del. C. =C2=
=A7 226(a)(3).</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When the Company did not res=
pond to the petition, the Investment Fund moved for a default judgment. The=
 motion emphasized that Braziel was a qualified person who had studied the =
rules governing receivers of Delaware corporations.</p><p style=3D"margin: =
16px 0;">By order dated November 29, 2016, the court entered a default judg=
ment and appointed Braziel as a receiver. The order charged Braziel with li=
quidating the Company and distributing its net assets to its investors. The=
 order did not authorize Braziel to conduct business through the Company.</=
p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Having cracked open the company=
, Braziel succeeded in getting money out of it. He sued Fund Alliance and g=
ot the web address back for $750,000. He renegotiated a settlement with Adv=
isorShares in which Fund.com got a couple of million dollars in exchange fo=
r canceling its AdvisorShares stake. Meanwhile, =E2=80=9Con=C2=A0paper, the=
 Company owed over $8 million to Galanis=E2=80=99s affiliates,=E2=80=9D and=
 Braziel succeeded in getting that debt canceled because Galanis forgot abo=
ut it:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">On December 22, [Braziel=
] moved for an order requiring that the Company=E2=80=99s creditors make an=
y claims against the receivership estate by April 14, 2017. The court appro=
ved the order and set a bar date for asserting claims. Galanis and his asso=
ciates failed to assert timely claims, which prevented them asserting any c=
laims based on their purported $8 million in debt. Braziel disallowed claim=
s for two other potential creditors for another $1.5 million.</p></blockquo=
te><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">All very nice work! Braziel now controlled =
a company that had little debt and some real money. What should he do with =
it? The good answer is =E2=80=9Creturn the money to the shareholders=C2=A0p=
ro rata, getting 20% himself.=E2=80=9D The actual answer is some combinatio=
n of =E2=80=9Ctake the money himself=E2=80=9D:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"m=
argin: 16px 0;">Over the next eight months, Braziel caused the Company to w=
ire a total of $121,000 to his personal checking account in eighteen instal=
lments. Braziel never disclosed the transfers to the court or his attorneys=
.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And =E2=80=9CYOLO some call =
options=E2=80=9D:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Braziel next =
decided to use the Company=E2=80=99s money to fund high-risk, high-reward i=
nvestments. Using a combination of money he extracted from the Company, plu=
s some funds received from family members, Braziel invested in common stock=
 and call options on the stock of Belmond, Ltd., a Bermudian hotelier. Betw=
een July 12 and 18, 2018, the Company wired $315,000 to Braziel=E2=80=99s c=
hecking account. On July 17, Braziel opened a brokerage account with Intera=
ctive Brokers, LLC (the =E2=80=9CFirst=C2=A0Brokerage Account=E2=80=9D). On=
 July 18 and 19, Braziel moved $270,000 from his checking account to the Fi=
rst Brokerage Account.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Starting on July 18,=
 2018, Braziel used the funds in the First Brokerage Account to buy and sel=
l common stock and call options for Belmond=E2=80=99s stock.</p></blockquot=
e><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This all went well enough that, =E2=80=9Chav=
ing seen that he could use the Company=E2=80=99s assets to make investments=
, Braziel filed a motion on October 16, 2018, that asked the court to disco=
ntinue the liquidation and approve the Company=E2=80=99s re-emergence as a =
publicly traded investment vehicle.=E2=80=9D The company is back in busines=
s. Also all of his trades were good? The call options turned out to be on a=
 merger target,=C2=A0and he sold <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusi=
ness.com/click/36159276.193394/aHR0cHM6Ly9mdW5kLmNvbS8/607f07ceef4b8524a319=
a23cB16605676" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_bl=
ank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; ba=
ckground-color: #ccc;">the web address</a> for more than he paid for it:</p=
><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Two months after the entry of the=
 Discontinuation Order, Braziel=E2=80=99s investment in Belmond paid off. T=
he bulk of the position consisted of out-of-the-money call options expiring=
 on December 21. Just in time, on December 14, LVMH Mo=C3=ABt Hennessy=E2=
=80=94Louis Vuitton SE announced an acquisition of Belmond=E2=80=99s public=
 equity at $25 per share. Braziel sold the call options for $936,344.77, ge=
nerating a gain of $832,464.61. In total, the value of the Belmond Investme=
nt amounted to approximately $1,494,337.06. Braziel also sold the Website A=
ddress. In December 2018, a third party agreed to buy the Website Address f=
or $1.5 million. After a 15% sales commission, the Company netted $1.275 mi=
llion, resulting in a gain of $525,000 over the $750,000 the Company paid t=
o re-acquire the Website Address in 2017.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margi=
n: 16px 0;">Good! But also bad:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"=
>Braziel helped himself to the proceeds. Between December 20, 2018, and Feb=
ruary 13, 2019, the Company wired $1.65 million to Braziel=E2=80=99s checki=
ng account. Braziel spent $347,786.43 on a sapphire ring and a pair of emer=
ald-and-diamond earrings from Lorraine Schwartz, $61,355.31 on a German wat=
ch, and over $400,000 on luxury hotel stays, apparel, art, and other fineri=
es. Braziel invested the rest of his takings in bankruptcy claims, cryptocu=
rrency, leveraged loans, and high-risk equities. Many of these investments =
produced outside gains. Despite having obtained the gains using Company fun=
ds, Braziel did not share the gains with the Company.=C2=A0</p></blockquote=
><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Eventually =E2=80=9Ca longtime stockholder of=
 the Company who spent part of his career as a federal prosecutor =E2=80=A6=
=C2=A0began to wonder what Braziel was doing.=E2=80=9D So he asked, did not=
 get a satisfactory answer, and sent a letter to the Delaware court that ha=
d supervised the liquidation, accusing Braziel of =E2=80=9Cembezzl[ing] thr=
ee million dollars from the shareholders.=E2=80=9D The court appointed a sp=
ecial magistrate to investigate, and the magistrate concluded that Braziel =
had in fact taken the money and should pay back about $2 million. Braziel w=
as apparently like =E2=80=9Cfair play, you got me=E2=80=9D:</p><blockquote>=
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">To the receiver=E2=80=99s [Braziel=E2=80=99s] =
credit, he largely acknowledged the accuracy of the special magistrate=E2=
=80=99s recommended factual findings. He also accepted aspects of the speci=
al magistrate=E2=80=99s recommended remedy by agreeing to provide restituti=
on and to pay the costs of the special magistrate=E2=80=99s investigation.<=
/p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">He raised some technical objec=
tions, though, and last week Delaware Vice Chancellor Travis Laster decided=
 he should actually pay back about $1.95 million. The quotes above are from=
 <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36159276.193394/a=
HR0cHM6Ly9jb3VydHMuZGVsYXdhcmUuZ292L09waW5pb25zL0Rvd25sb2FkLmFzcHg_aWQ9MzY2=
NzYw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB1af0524d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D=
"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: non=
e !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; borde=
r-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Vice Chancellor Laster=E2=
=80=99s opinion</a>.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suppose the moral of=
 the story is something like: If you go around hunting for defunct scams th=
at you can take over and sell for parts, you might be tempted to keep all t=
he money for yourself. =E2=80=9CAhh, this thing was a scam anyway=E2=80=9D =

5px;">M&A fees</h2>
</td>

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Investment bankers mostly work for fre=
e, and are sometimes lavishly overpaid. If you are the chief executive offi=
cer of a big company, and you want advice about potential acquisition targe=
ts, or modeling for potential financing transactions, or a recommendation f=
or a new chief financial officer, you can call up your friendly investment =
banker and say =E2=80=9Chey no rush or anything but I was wondering about .=
..=E2=80=9D and she will send you a beautifully formatted 40-page analysis =
by the next morning. You would never think of paying for this, and she woul=
d never ask. But then if you ever=C2=A0<em>do=C2=A0</em>acquire a company o=
r raise financing, you will feel some obligation to call her, and she will =
charge you millions of dollars for <em>that</em>.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">This works fine, but it has to be carefully calibrated. If a b=
ank builds its business on a model like =E2=80=9C10% of our clients will pu=
rsue deals, and 50% of those deals will sign, and 80% of those deals will c=
lose,=E2=80=9D then it can figure that it will get paid for 4% of its work =
and charge 25 times as much as that work costs, to cover all of the unpaid =
work.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: =
transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[3]</span>
</a> But then if the antitrust regime changes and only 60% of signed deals =
close, you are underpaid.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">At Reuters, <a hr=
ef=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36159276.193394/aHR0cHM=
6Ly93d3cucmV1dGVycy5jb20vYnVzaW5lc3MvZmluYW5jZS93aXRoLW5vLWJpZy1kZWFsLXNhZm=

7ceef4b8524a319a23cB26eb5583" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope=
" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importa=
nt; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-s=
tyle: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Anirban Sen reports</a>:</p><blockquo=
te><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bankers have been pushing to get paid even =
when a deal is thwarted by regulators, and are charging more for services p=
aid irrespective of whether a transaction closes, interviews with more than=
 a dozen dealmakers showed.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The banks' tact=
ics include taking a larger slice of the breakup fee paid by the acquirer t=
o the target for failing to close a deal, and charging more for "fairness o=
pinions" they provide to companies on whether they should sell themselves. =
=E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">U.S. antitrust regulators filed 5=
0 enforcement actions against mergers in the 12 months to the end of Septem=
ber 2022, representing the highest level of enforcement activity in over 20=
 years, according to the most recent data published by the Federal Trade Co=
mmission and U.S. Department of Justice. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 1=
6px 0;">Top investment banks, including Goldman Sachs (GS.N), opens new tab=
, JPMorgan Chase (JPM.N), opens new tab and Morgan Stanley (MS.N), opens ne=
w tab, are pushing to be paid as much as 25% of the breakup fee on some tra=
nsactions, depending on the transaction's size, according to the dealmakers=
 who were interviewed. That is up from a historic average of receiving abou=
t 15% of the breakup fee, they added. =E2=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px=
 0;">Investment banks have also been making roughly 20-25% of their advisor=
y fees to companies selling themselves subject to delivering fairness opini=
ons, which are paid even if a deal does not close. Referred to in the indus=
try as "announcement" fees, these are up from an average of 5% to 6% of the=
 total advisory fees during the previous decade, according to several dealm=
akers and regulatory filings.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=
It would be funny if the bankers explicitly said =E2=80=9Cthe only way we c=
an keep our prices low is by also charging for busted deals,=E2=80=9D but t=
hey don=E2=80=99t, probably because it=E2=80=99s hard to argue that their p=
rices are low.</p><table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cells=

</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A few months ago, <a href=3D"https://l=

ll Ackman=E2=80=99s old-school approach to managing a hedge fund =E2=80=94 =
he=E2=80=99s a famous guy, he makes concentrated high-conviction bets on st=
ocks, he doesn=E2=80=99t hedge much,=E2=80=9D and a reader wrote in to chas=
tise me. The=C2=A0<em>old-school=C2=A0</em>approach to managing a hedge fun=
d is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/36159276.1933=

Jones invented the hedge fund, and his early approach was arguably more lik=

ick stocks and hedge out market exposure. (Thus the name.) Jones also inven=
ted the hedge fund compensation scheme, though (particularly the 20% perfor=
mance fee), and=C2=A0<em>that </em>proliferated among funds that didn=E2=80=
=99t do as much hedging. But a famous guy making big stock bets and chargin=
g 20% of returns was,=C2=A0in the long history of hedge funds, something of=
 a novelty.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still, I stand by what I wrote:=
 There <em>was=C2=A0</em>a fairly long vogue for high-profile individual he=
dge fund managers who made big bets, and that era is passing, and now hedge=
 funds are big institutions stocked with well-paid but somewhat anonymous p=
ortfolio managers grinding out alpha with low volatility. At Business Insid=
er, Linette Lopez <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=

</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What is clear is that hed=
ge funds run by an individual star, by one prodigious mind, are not raising=
 the massive money they used to. Clients who once were proud to hand their =
money to a specific person are pushing back; they want to pay lower fees an=
d see less-volatile returns. The funds that have survived rely on a stable =
of faceless traders testing out different ideas, brokering transactions, an=
d harvesting the returns for the collective. Quant strategies =E2=80=94 whi=
ch are built on algorithmic trading =E2=80=94 have also become more popular=
 with the ultrawealthy. More robots, fewer people, lower overhead. =E2=80=
=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In decades past, clients were mostly HN=
WIs: high-net-worth individuals. Nowadays the biggest money runners are ins=
titutions, and institutions are more exacting. One legendary but shy fund m=
anager told me that these institutions are more interested in steady return=
s, and the wild swings in performance that hedge funds offer simply give th=
em heartburn. "They can sleep better at night," the person said. "The econo=
my can turn bad, the stock market can crash, and they're not going to share=
 losses."</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Sebastian Mallaby, the author of =
"More Money Than God: Hedge Funds and the Making of the New Elite," told me=
 that institutions are also less willing to be enchanted by the personal ma=
gnetism of a founder like Julian Robertson, the founder of the legendary fu=
nd Tiger Management.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">"In the old days, Juli=
an Robertson would hold a party for HNWIs and have some kind of wacky show =
for them at the Met, and they'd say 'Julian, what a guy' and leave him alon=
e for a year," Mallaby said. "That's gone away because individual investors=
 aren't as important as institutional investors, who have to show they've b=
een careful where they put the money.=E2=80=9D</p></blockquote><p style=3D"=
margin: 16px 0;">The story here is something like =E2=80=9Cinstitutions dem=
and that they actually get identifiable alpha and uncorrelated, high and st=
able returns in exchange for all the fees they are paying hedge funds, and =
the hedge fund industry is responding by building stable professional insti=
tutions that actually provide that service.=E2=80=9D That=E2=80=99s a good =
story! But the parties might be less fun.</p><table class=3D"header" width=

bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">Wall Street Journal article</=
a> about Amazon.com=E2=80=99s smart speaker business:</p><blockquote><p sty=
le=3D"margin: 16px 0;">When Amazon launched the Echo smart home devices wit=
h its Alexa voice assistant in 2014, it pulled a page from shaving giant Gi=
llette=E2=80=99s classic playbook: sell the razors for a pittance in the ho=
pe of making heaps of money on purchases of the refill blades.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A decade later, the payoff for Echo hasn=E2=80=99t arr=
ived. While hundreds of millions of customers have Alexa-enabled devices, t=
he idea that people would spend meaningful amounts of money to buy goods on=
 Amazon by talking to the iconic voice assistant on the underpriced speaker=
s didn=E2=80=99t take off.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Customers actual=
ly used Echo mostly for free apps such as setting alarms and checking the w=
eather. =E2=80=9CWe worried we=E2=80=99ve hired 10,000 people and we=E2=80=
=99ve built a smart timer,=E2=80=9D said a former senior employee.=C2=A0=E2=
=80=A6</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As [Chief Executive Officer Andy] Ja=
ssy tries to fix it, he is rethinking the obscure Bezos-era metric inside A=
mazon that helps explain why Echo and other devices could accrue such huge =
losses for so long with little repercussion. Called =E2=80=9Cdownstream imp=
act,=E2=80=9D or DSI, it assigns a financial value to a product or a servic=
e based on how customers spend within Amazon=E2=80=99s ecosystem after they=
 buy it. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The metric was developed in 20=
11 by a team of economists including an eventual Nobel Prize winner. In som=
e instances, the model worked clearly. When customers buy Amazon=E2=80=99s =
Kindle e-reader=E2=80=94one of Amazon=E2=80=99s profitable devices=E2=80=94=
they are very likely to then buy ebooks to read on that device. Ebooks are =
part of the books business, not the devices business, but Amazon leaders sa=
id it made sense for the Kindle team to claim part of revenue when assessin=
g their product=E2=80=99s internal value. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0=
;">In other cases=E2=80=94especially Echo devices=E2=80=94the downstream im=
pact idea broke down, said the people familiar with the devices business.=
=C2=A0</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you were in the busi=
ness of selling smart speakers, you would try to (1) sell them for more tha=
n it cost to make them and/or (2) negotiate some sort of arm=E2=80=99s-leng=
th partnerships with advertisers, business partners, etc., who might pay yo=
u for access to your smart speakers=E2=80=99 customers. If the amount of mo=
ney that customers paid, plus the amount of money that advertisers/partners=
 paid, exceeded the cost of making the speaker, you=E2=80=99d do it. If not=
, you=E2=80=99d stop. Capitalism!</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But Amazo=
n is not in the business of selling smart speakers. It=E2=80=99s in the bus=
iness of selling=C2=A0<em>everything</em>, which is harder. If you sell eve=
rything, your price signals get obscured. If Amazon just gave everyone a ph=
one, for free, with the Amazon app on it, that would probably increase Amaz=
on=E2=80=99s sales of books and toilet paper and electronics and dog food a=
nd video subscriptions. But would it be worth it? For that you need a model=
 developed by a team of economists. And sometimes the model doesn=E2=80=99t=
 work.</p><table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=
